one_line_takeaway: Introduces an Attack-Defense-Tree + CVSS-v3.1 framework that decomposes LLM-system attack paths into precondition/execution/impact layers with a majority-Attack-Complexity propagation rule, and uses it to show, on a healthcare case study, that hardening all preconditions (Epath 3.89->1.62) beats single-leaf or execution-only hardening (->2.22) for the dominant prompt-injection path, though absolute CVSS base scores cluster near 7.5 across most branches due to the framework's own modeling assumptions.
key_finding: The G1 prompt-injection branch (B1) baseline: five OR'd preconditions give E(P)=max(2.84,3.89,2.84,2.07,2.22)=3.89 with ACmaj(P)=L; direct/indirect execution under the shared conditioned vector also yields E(V*)=3.89, so Epath(B1)=min(3.89,3.89)=3.89; combined with G1's integrity-only impact (C,I,A)=(0,0.56,0), Impact=3.60, giving Base=7.5 (High). Treatment scenarios on this same path: S1 (harden a single precondition) yields only a limited reduction because OR aggregation is still dominated by the remaining network-reachable leaves; S2 (harden all preconditions) raises ACmaj(P) to H and drops Epath to 1.62; S3 (execution-only guardrails, preconditions untouched) drops Epath to 2.22; S4 (adds HITL gating, UI:N->R) does not improve on S2's Epath because the SAND composition is still bounded by E(P), even though E(V*) itself falls -- illustrating that the SAND min-operator identifies the true bottleneck and that stacking controls on an already-hardened side yields diminishing returns. A broader finding (Discussion) is that path-level base scores cluster tightly around 7.5 across many distinct branches in G1-G3, which the authors attribute to two deliberate modeling choices (near-uniform AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N for internet-reachable LLM surfaces, and a single dominant CIA dimension per goal) rather than to a lack of resolution in the framework -- they argue the ADT decomposition, not the absolute score, is what differentiates operationally distinct paths (e.g., B1 vs B3 both score 7.5 under G1 but concentrate risk in different subsystems).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (distinct from the two prior papers by the same authors it builds on, [9] system modeling/STRIDE catalog and [10] high-level attack-tree prioritization) is a goal-driven risk-assessment methodology for LLM-powered systems that: (1) constructs Attack-Defense Trees (ADTs) per security goal, decomposing every attack path into Precondition (P), Execution (V), and goal-level Impact, using OR/AND/SAND connectors; (2) attaches CVSS v3.1 exploitability vectors (AV, AC, PR, UI) to ADT leaves via representative CVEs, reserves impact (C,I,A) for the goal node only, and propagates exploitability through the tree with a novel 'majority Attack Complexity' (ACmaj) rule that conditions the execution step's AC on which preconditions were actually satisfied, to avoid double-counting AV/PR/UI; (3) a risk-treatment workflow that models concrete defenses as transformations of the CVSS base metrics at specific leaves, paired with a 4-level ordinal cost scale (engineering effort, infra footprint, ops load, UX impact), and four canonical hardening scenarios (precondition-only, all-preconditions, execution-only/guardrails, combined). The method is demonstrated end-to-end on a healthcare LLM-assistant case study.
threat_model: Three system-level security goals for an LLM-powered healthcare assistant (web app + orchestrator/agent layer with Task Planner/Task Executor/Data Pipeline + LLM + external tools + EHR platform): G1 intervening in medical procedures (unsafe/manipulated clinical guidance, integrity), G2 leakage of EHR data (confidentiality), G3 disruption of access/availability. Attacker capability is expressed per-leaf via CVSS v3.1 metrics (Attack Vector, Attack Complexity, Privileges Required, User Interaction), reused from representative real CVEs (e.g., CVE-2025-22222 for user hijacking, CVE-2025-22457/53770/32433 for user-machine hijack, CVE-2024-12012 for stolen API keys, CVE-2024-50691 for web-session MiTM) or, where no CVE exists for an LLM-specific technique, by analogical mapping to CVEs with comparable attack surface (e.g., KV-cache leakage modeled after PromptPeek-style cache isolation failures). Threats are grouped into conventional cyber (MitM, credential theft), adversarial ML (model poisoning/replacement/extraction, membership inference, training-data extraction/inversion), and conversational (direct/indirect prompt injection, session hijacking/fixation, orchestration task injection/memory poisoning/agent-in-the-middle, prompt flooding/resource exhaustion DoS).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) CVSS v3.1 has no native metric for LLM-service-specific phenomena such as cost-amplification/prompt-flooding, recursive agent/tool loops, or multi-tenant KV-cache artifacts; these must be approximated via proxy CVE analogies, which compresses distinct LLM attack families into similar exploitability values and reduces ranking resolution (one prompt-flooding leaf was approximated by aligning with emerging CVSS v4.0 semantics while still reporting a v3.1 vector). (2) The observed clustering of base scores near 7.5 could superficially look like a lack of granularity (acknowledged and explained but not eliminated). (3) The framework is demonstrated on a single healthcare case study with no external/independent validation against real incident data or red-team results; representative-CVE selection and analogical mapping for LLM-specific leaves involve subjective judgment that the authors document but do not blind-validate. (4) Cost levels are ordinal, not monetary, so cross-organization comparability is limited. (5) The paper notes CVSS v4.0 would better suit multi-tenant, service-heavy LLM architectures and flags porting the same ADT structure to v4.0 as future work rather than something done here.

quantitative_results: G1-B1 (prompt injection) baseline: E(P)=3.89, ACmaj(P)=L, E(V*)=3.89, Epath=3.89, Impact=3.60 (C,I,A)=(0,0.56,0), Base=7.5

G1-B1 precondition leaf exploitabilities (baseline -> post-control): user hijacking 2.84->1.23 (MFA, PR:L->H) ->0.71 combined with short-lived tokens (AC:L->H); user-machine hijack 3.89->2.22 (EDR/OS hardening, AC:L->H) ->1.62 with device binding (PR:N->L); malicious user 2.84->1.23 (RBAC, PR:L->H); stolen API key 2.07->0.90 (scope limiting, PR:L->H); web-session MiTM 2.22->1.62 (TLS1.3+pinning, PR:N->L) ->0.71 (mTLS, PR:L->H)

G1-B1 treatment scenarios: S1 (single precondition hardened) limited reduction; S2 (all preconditions hardened) Epath=1.62; S3 (execution-only guardrails) Epath=2.22; S4 (S2 + HITL gating, UI:N->R) no further Epath improvement over S2 despite E(V*) decreasing, because SAND is bounded by E(P)

Path-level CVSS base scores cluster near 7.5 (High) across most G1-G3 branches (e.g., G1 Prompt Injection and Orchestration Errors both 7.5; G2 User Session Compromise, LLM Session Compromise, and Model Extraction all 7.5), attributed to near-uniform AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N leaf profiles and single-dominant-CIA-dimension goal impacts rather than framework insensitivity
